notes: Base class for all Unity scripts. Inherit to get lifecycle methods (awake, start, update, etc.).
behavioral_differences: Unity uses reflection to find lifecycle methods; Python version uses inheritance and method override
Unity supports partial classes; Python does not

notes: Container for components. Created with GameObject('name'). Has global registry for Find/FindWithTag.
behavioral_differences: Unity GameObjects exist in scenes; Python uses a flat global registry
Unity has SetActive(); Python uses .active bool directly

notes: Every GameObject has a Transform. Stores position (Vector3), rotation (Quaternion), scale. Supports parent-child hierarchy.
behavioral_differences: Unity Transform has localPosition/localRotation separate from world; Python stores world position directly
Position setter accepts Vector2 (converted to Vector3 with z=0) for 2D convenience

notes: 2D physics body wrapping pymunk.Body. Supports dynamic, kinematic, and static body types.
example_unity:
Rigidbody2D rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>();
rb.velocity = new Vector2(5, 0);
behavioral_differences: Unity uses Box2D internally; Python uses pymunk (Chipmunk). Collision resolution differs slightly
Unity drag maps approximately to pymunk damping

notes: Box-shaped 2D collider. Must call build() after setting size and attaching to GameObject.
example_unity:
BoxCollider2D col = gameObject.AddComponent<BoxCollider2D>();
col.size = new Vector2(2, 1);
behavioral_differences: Python requires explicit build() call; Unity auto-builds colliders
Trigger detection uses pymunk sensor shapes

notes: Circle-shaped 2D collider. Must call build() after setting radius.
behavioral_differences: Same as BoxCollider2D — requires explicit build()

notes: Orthographic camera for 2D. First camera created becomes Camera.main. Handles world-to-screen conversion.
behavioral_differences: Only orthographic mode supported (no perspective)
Unity uses orthographicSize (camelCase); Python uses orthographic_size (snake_case)

notes: Renders a sprite or colored rectangle. Falls back to shape drawing when no sprite assigned.
behavioral_differences: Unity Color is a struct (0-1 floats); Python uses RGB tuple (0-255 ints)
No sprite atlas or animation support

notes: 2D vector backed by numpy. Supports arithmetic, dot, distance, lerp, angle. Static constructors: Vector2.zero, Vector2.up, etc.
example_unity:
Vector2 v = new Vector2(3, 4);
float mag = v.magnitude;

notes: 3D vector backed by numpy. Supports cross product, project, reflect. Static constructors: Vector3.zero, Vector3.forward, etc.
example_unity:
Vector3 v = new Vector3(1, 2, 3);
Vector3 n = v.normalized;

notes: Rotation quaternion backed by pyrr. Supports euler conversion, angle-axis, slerp.
behavioral_differences: Unity Quaternion.Euler is PascalCase; Python is snake_case

notes: Static math utilities. Lerp, Clamp, Approximately, SmoothStep, etc.
Controls bounciness and friction for 2D colliders. Assign to Collider2D.shared_material or .material. Unity defaults: bounciness=0, friction=0.4.
behavioral_differences: Unity PhysicsMaterial2D is a ScriptableObject asset; Python version is a plain object
pymunk uses 'elasticity' for bounciness — mapped automatically

notes: Static class for 2D physics queries: Raycast, OverlapCircle, OverlapBox. Uses pymunk spatial queries.
example_unity:
RaycastHit2D hit = Physics2D.Raycast(origin, direction, distance);
Collider2D col = Physics2D.OverlapCircle(point, radius);
example_python:
hit = Physics2D.raycast(origin, direction, distance)
col = Physics2D.overlap_circle(point, radius)
behavioral_differences: pymunk uses segment_query for raycasts; results may differ slightly from Box2D
layer_mask parameter accepted but not yet filtered

notes: Data class returned by Physics2D.Raycast. Contains point, normal, distance, collider, rigidbody, transform.
example_unity:
RaycastHit2D hit;
if (Physics2D.Raycast(origin, dir, out hit)) { Debug.Log(hit.point); }
example_python:
hit = Physics2D.raycast(origin, dir)
if hit: print(hit.point)
behavioral_differences: Unity RaycastHit2D is a struct; Python version is a dataclass

notes: Static class for logging and visual debugging. Log, LogWarning, LogError for console output. DrawLine, DrawRay for visual debug.
behavioral_differences: Unity Debug.Log outputs to Unity Console; Python prints to stdout/stderr
Debug.DrawLine renders via pygame overlay, not Unity Gizmos

notes: Wraps an audio file. Created with AudioClip('path/to/file.wav'). Unity version is an asset.
behavioral_differences: Unity AudioClip is a serialized asset; Python loads from file path at runtime

notes: Component for playing audio. Supports play, stop, pause, loop, volume, pitch, PlayOneShot.
behavioral_differences: Unity AudioSource has spatial audio (3D sound); Python version is 2D only via pygame.mixer
Pitch control not implemented in pygame backend

notes: Marks a GameObject as the audio listener. Only one active at a time. Typically on the main camera.
example_unity: // Usually added via Unity Inspector on Camera
behavioral_differences: Unity AudioListener handles 3D spatialization; Python version is a marker component only

notes: Root container for UI elements. Supports ScreenSpaceOverlay render mode. All UI elements should be children of a Canvas.
behavioral_differences: Only ScreenSpaceOverlay mode implemented
No Canvas Scaler or Graphic Raycaster

notes: UI-specific transform with anchoring, pivot, and sizeDelta. Used instead of Transform for UI elements.
example_unity:
RectTransform rt = GetComponent<RectTransform>();
rt.sizeDelta = new Vector2(200, 50);
behavioral_differences: Simplified anchor/pivot model; no stretch anchors or layout groups

notes: UI text display. Properties: text, fontSize, color, alignment.
behavioral_differences: Unity Text uses rich text markup; Python version is plain text only
Font selection not implemented (uses pygame default)

notes: UI image/panel component with color and sprite properties.
behavioral_differences: No image type (Simple, Sliced, Tiled, Filled)
No fill amount or ray cast target

notes: UI button with click callback and interactable toggle.
behavioral_differences: Unity Button uses UnityEvent; Python uses a simple callable
No transition animations (color tint, sprite swap)

notes: Scene loading and management. Register scenes with setup callables, load by name or index.
behavioral_differences: Unity loads scenes from build settings; Python uses registered callables
No async scene loading (LoadSceneAsync)
No additive scene loading

notes: Property animation system. Tween.to() animates to target, supports easing, looping, sequences.
behavioral_differences: DOTween uses extension methods on Transform/Material; Python uses Tween.to(target, property, ...)
No DOVirtual, DOPath, or shader property tweening yet
Sequence API matches DOTween: append, join, on_complete
